meta.siteDescription: We build turnkey websites for experts, businesses and e-commerce that help attract leads, sell services and convert traffic into clients.
meta.ogTitle: NAKO Agency | Turnkey Websites for Business & Experts
meta.ogDescription: Strategy, design and development in one solution. Websites for experts, businesses, e-commerce, courses and events.
meta.keywords: website development, web design, web studio, turnkey website, landing page, online store, expert website, business website, app development

items.title: RESULTS
items.subtitle: Websites for business results
items.description: We build websites not for beauty, but for leads, sales and trust. Every element works towards conversion and commercial results.

items.title: FULL CYCLE
items.subtitle: From brief to support
items.description: Strategy, structure, copy, design, development, content, integrations, launch and support — all in one place. No need to find separate contractors.

items.title: PRESENTATION
items.subtitle: Premium design + sales logic
items.description: We combine stylish visual presentation with a well-thought-out structure and conversion logic. Premium, calm, modern — without unnecessary noise.

items.title: INTEGRATIONS
items.subtitle: We integrate your website into business processes
items.description: CRM, Telegram, email, analytics, payments — we connect everything needed so your website works as part of your system, not just a business card.

items.title: SUPPORT
items.subtitle: We stay after launch
items.description: We don't abandon you after delivery. Bugfixes, minor edits, consultations, monthly support — choose a convenient support format.

items.title: INDIVIDUAL APPROACH
items.subtitle: No templates or copies
items.description: We don't use a one-size-fits-all strategy. Every project is a unique solution built for your goals, audience and business model.

items.label: conversion boost
items.description: through thoughtful structure, messaging and UX

items.label: integrations
items.description: CRM, Telegram, analytics, payments

items.title: Expert websites
items.description: Websites for experts, consultants, mentors and specialists who need a strong personal brand and a flow of leads.

items.title: Business websites
items.description: Websites for companies, studios and local businesses to present services, build trust and attract clients.

items.title: Landing pages
items.description: Landing pages for services, products, advertising, launches and special offers.

items.title: Online stores
items.description: User-friendly online stores with well-thought-out structure, catalog, product cards and purchase logic.

items.title: Course websites
items.description: Websites for educational projects, online courses and schools with clear structure and strong presentation.

items.title: Event websites
items.description: Websites for events, conferences, presentations, workshops and special projects.

steps.title: Strategy & Brief
steps.description: We analyze your goals, audience, and positioning. Fill out the brief, define website objectives and key result metrics.

steps.title: Structure & Prototype
steps.description: We design site architecture, user scenarios, and wireframes. Align on logic and navigation before moving to design.

steps.title: Design
steps.description: We create an individual visual style. Every screen is a thoughtful composition with attention to typography and detail.

steps.title: Development & Integrations
steps.description: We build on Next.js with focus on speed and SEO. Connect CRM, Telegram, email, analytics, and payments.

steps.title: Content & Copy
steps.description: We fill the site with content: text, photos, media. Help with messaging, copywriting, and visual selection.

steps.title: Testing
steps.description: We test on all devices and browsers. Check forms, integrations, user scenarios, and performance.

steps.title: Launch
steps.description: We deploy to production, set up domain, SSL, and analytics. Hand over access, documentation, and train you on site management.

steps.title: Support
steps.description: We stay in touch after launch. Bug fixes, updates, minor edits, and project development within a support package.

items.name: Anna Kovaleva
items.quote: The website paid for itself in the first month. Bookings tripled, and clients come already 'warm' — they've read everything about me on the site.

items.name: Maxim Petrov
items.quote: Finally the store works fast. Before, clients left because of slow loading. Now conversion has grown by 40%.

items.name: Elena Sokolova
items.quote: Really liked the approach — not just a pretty picture, but a thought-out structure. Every block works for conversion.

items.name: Dmitry Volkov
items.quote: Launched the landing in 2 weeks. Quality on par with top agencies, but without their budgets and bureaucracy.

items.name: Olga Mironova
items.quote: The website captures the studio atmosphere perfectly. Minimalism, space, calm — exactly what was needed.

faq.caption: Answers
faq.title: FAQ
faq.ctaSubtitle: Contact us — we'll reply within an hour

items.question: How long does development take?
items.answer: Timelines depend on project complexity. Landing page — up to 14 days, expert website — up to 21 days, online store — up to 45 days. Exact timelines are discussed after filling out the brief.

items.question: How much does a website cost? What affects the price?
items.answer: Cost depends on website type, number of pages, design complexity, and integrations. Exact cost is calculated individually after filling out the brief.

items.question: What's included in turnkey development?
items.answer: Full cycle: brief and analytics, prototyping, unique design, responsive coding, basic SEO, analytics setup, testing and launch. You get a website ready to work.

items.question: Do I need a domain and hosting?
items.answer: Domain needs to be registered in advance or we'll help with selection and registration. Hosting — we use modern platforms (Vercel, Netlify) with free tiers for most projects.

items.question: How many revisions are included?
items.answer: Each stage includes a round of revisions: prototype, design, coding. Minor corrections — unlimited. Major concept changes are quoted separately.

items.question: Is there support after launch?
items.answer: Yes, 30 days of free support after launch: bug fixes, minor edits, consultations. After that — by support agreement or on-demand.

items.question: Will the website be SEO-optimized?
items.answer: Basic SEO is included: semantic markup, meta tags, sitemap, robots.txt, image optimization, loading speed. Promotion and content marketing — separate service.

items.question: What integrations are possible?
items.answer: Any: CRM (amoCRM, Bitrix24), payment systems (Stripe, PayPal), email newsletters, Telegram bots, booking calendars, analytics (GA4, etc.) and others on request.

items.question: How does payment work?
items.answer: In stages: 50% prepayment before starting, 50% after completion and acceptance. For large projects, splitting into 3 stages is possible. We work by contract.

items.question: What if I don't like the design?
items.answer: We approve each stage before moving to the next. If the design doesn't suit after the first iteration — we offer 2 alternative concepts. In case of complete disagreement — refund per contract.

ru152fz.title: Consent to Personal Data Processing
sections: I, the data subject, hereby freely, voluntarily and in my own interest give my consent to NAKO Agency (hereinafter — the Operator) for the processing of my personal data in accordance with Federal Law No. 152-FZ of 27.07.2006 'On Personal Data'.

The following personal data is subject to processing: surname, first name, email address, telephone number, messenger username, and other data voluntarily provided by me when filling out forms on the website.
Purposes of personal data processing: processing incoming requests; contacting the data subject to discuss a project; sending informational and commercial messages (with separate consent).

Processing methods: collection, recording, systematisation, accumulation, storage, clarification, extraction, use, transfer (provision, access), anonymisation, blocking, deletion, destruction of personal data — with and without the use of automation tools.
Validity period: from the moment of provision until withdrawal. Consent may be withdrawn by sending a written notification to hello@nakoagency.com.
The Operator undertakes to ensure the security of personal data and take necessary measures to prevent unauthorised access.

about.metaDescription: A web studio focused on results. We build websites for experts, e-commerce and business. Learn about our approach, values and work principles.
about.heroDescription: We are an agency that builds modern turnkey websites for experts, businesses and digital projects, combining strategy, design and development in one solution. Our goal is to make websites not just visually beautiful, but commercially strong and working for leads.
about.approachText1: We build websites not for the sake of having one, but for business results. Every project starts with understanding your goals and audience.
about.approachText2: Full cycle: from brief and messaging to launch and support. Strategy → Structure → Design → Development → Content → Launch.
about.approachText3: Premium visual presentation with sales and conversion logic. We remove the unnecessary to leave what converts visitors into clients.

values.title: Results
values.description: A website is a tool. Beauty without conversion is meaningless. We create what works.

values.title: Transparency
values.description: Fixed timelines, clear stages, regular reports. You always know where the project stands.

values.title: Quality
values.description: Clean code, fast loading, SEO out of the box. We do it right so it doesn't need redoing.

values.title: Partnership
values.description: Not contractors, but partners. We advise, suggest, and debate — if it's in the project's interest.

principles.title: Strategy first
principles.description: We don't start design until we understand goals and audience.

principles.title: Less is more
principles.description: Every element must work. Decoration for decoration's sake is not our way.

principles.title: Mobile-first
principles.description: 60%+ traffic is mobile. Phone first, then desktop.

principles.title: Speed is critical
principles.description: Lighthouse 90+ is the standard. Fast site = more conversions.

principles.title: SEO from day one
principles.description: Not 'we'll fix it later', but right away: semantics, meta tags, structure.

principles.title: Support included
principles.description: 30 days after launch. Bugs, edits, questions — we handle it.

privacy.metaDescription: NAKO Agency privacy policy. Information about collection, use and protection of personal data.
privacy.caption: Legal information

sections.title: General Provisions
content: NAKO Agency ("we", "our", "Company") acts as the data controller for personal data collected through this website.
Website: nakoagency.com.
This Privacy Policy applies to all users worldwide, regardless of location.
We operate across the following jurisdictions: Moldova, Romania, Ukraine, Poland, Germany, Italy, Russia, and Kazakhstan.

Applicable data protection regulations include: the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) for the European Union; Law No. 195/2024 on Personal Data Protection (Moldova); Law No. 2297-VI on Personal Data Protection (Ukraine); Federal Law No. 152-FZ on Personal Data (Russia); Law No. 94-V on Personal Data and Their Protection (Kazakhstan).

sections.title: Data We Collect
content: Personal data provided through forms: name, email address, phone number, Telegram username, company name, and project description.
Automatically collected data: IP address (anonymized for EU users), browser type, device information, pages visited, and referrer URL.
Cookies: we use necessary, analytics, marketing, and preference cookies. For details, please see our Cookie Policy.
Third-party services that may collect data: Google Analytics 4 and Vercel Analytics.

sections.title: Purposes and Legal Basis
content: Processing form submissions — legal basis: your consent (Art. 6(1)(a) GDPR).
Communication about projects and services — legal basis: contractual necessity (Art. 6(1)(b) GDPR).
Website improvement and analytics — legal basis: legitimate interest (Art. 6(1)(f) GDPR).
Legal compliance — legal basis: legal obligation (Art. 6(1)(c) GDPR).
Marketing communications (only with your explicit consent) — legal basis: consent (Art. 6(1)(a) GDPR).

sections.title: Cookies and Tracking
content: We use the following categories of cookies: necessary (always active), analytics (opt-in), marketing (opt-in), and preferences (opt-in).
Google Consent Mode v2 is implemented on our website.
Before you provide consent, no non-essential cookies are set.
You can manage your cookie preferences via the cookie banner displayed on your first visit, or through the "Cookie Settings" link in the website footer at any time.
For full details on the cookies we use, their purposes, and retention periods, please see our Cookie Policy page.

sections.title: Data Sharing
content: We do NOT sell your personal data under any circumstances.

We share data with the following service providers (data processors): Vercel Inc. (hosting, United States — protected by EU Standard Contractual Clauses); Resend Inc. (email delivery, United States — protected by EU Standard Contractual Clauses); Google LLC (analytics, United States — protected by EU Standard Contractual Clauses).
We may disclose data to legal authorities upon lawful request.
All third-party processors are bound by Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) that ensure an adequate level of data protection.

sections.title: International Data Transfers
content: Our website is hosted on the Vercel Inc. platform (USA). When you submit a form, your data is processed in transit by Vercel servers located in the USA and EU, and immediately forwarded to our email and Telegram. Personal data is not stored on Vercel servers or in any databases.
By submitting a form, you consent to the cross-border transfer of your data to the specified jurisdictions.
Third-party services and their locations: Vercel Inc. (USA/EU) — hosting and transit form processing; Google LLC (USA) — analytics (Google Analytics 4); Resend Inc. (USA) — email delivery.
Safeguards for EU residents: data transfers to the USA are based on EU Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) concluded between us and our service providers. All data is encrypted in transit (TLS 1.2+).

For Russian residents (152-FZ): personal data submitted through website forms is processed in transit and is not stored on servers outside the Russian Federation. Data is forwarded to the operator's email and messenger. By submitting a form, you confirm your consent to cross-border data transfer in accordance with Art. 12 of Federal Law No. 152-FZ.

For Kazakh residents (Law No. 94-V): personal data is processed in transit and is not stored on servers. By submitting a form, you confirm your consent to cross-border data transfer in accordance with the legislation of the Republic of Kazakhstan on personal data.
For residents of Moldova, Ukraine, Poland, Germany, Italy, and Romania: safeguards provided by the GDPR (EU Regulation 2016/679) and the respective national legislation apply.

sections.title: Data Retention
content: Form submissions: retained for 2 years from the date of submission, then permanently deleted.
Analytics data: retained for 14 months (Google Analytics 4 standard retention period).
Cookies: retention varies by type; see our Cookie Policy for specific durations.
Cookie consent records: retained for 3 years as proof of GDPR compliance.
Upon your request: personal data will be deleted within 30 days.

sections.title: Your Rights
content: All users have the right to: access their personal data, request rectification of inaccurate data, request deletion of their data, and withdraw consent to data processing.

EU residents (under GDPR) additionally have the right to: data portability, restriction of processing, objection to processing, and the right not to be subject to automated decision-making. Relevant supervisory authorities: ANSPDCP (Romania), UODO (Poland), BfDI (Germany), Garante per la protezione dei dati personali (Italy).
Russian residents (under 152-FZ) have the right to: access, correction, blocking, and destruction of personal data, as well as withdrawal of consent. Supervisory authority: Roskomnadzor.
Kazakh residents (under Law No. 94-V) have the right to: access, correction, and deletion of personal data. Contact: Committee on Information Security of the Ministry of Digital Development.
Moldovan residents (under Law No. 195/2024) will have GDPR-equivalent rights from August 2026. Supervisory authority: National Centre for Personal Data Protection (NCPDP).

Ukrainian residents (under Law No. 2297-VI) have the right to: access, correction, deletion of personal data, and withdrawal of consent. Supervisory authority: Ukrainian Parliament Commissioner for Human Rights (Ombudsman of Ukraine).
To exercise any of your rights, please contact us at: hello@nakoagency.com. We will respond within 30 days of receiving your request.

sections.title: Data Security
content: All data is encrypted in transit using HTTPS/TLS protocols.
Access to personal data is restricted to authorized personnel only.
We conduct regular security reviews of our systems and processes.
While no method of electronic transmission or storage is 100% secure, in the event of a data breach we will notify affected users within 72 hours as required by the GDPR.

sections.title: Children's Privacy
content: This website is not intended for use by children.
Age thresholds for consent vary by jurisdiction: under 16 in Germany and the Netherlands; under 14 in Italy; under 13 in Poland and by default in other jurisdictions.
We do not knowingly collect personal data from children. If you believe a child has provided us with personal data, please contact us immediately.

sections.title: Changes to This Policy
content: We may update this Privacy Policy from time to time. Changes become effective upon publication on the website.
In the event of material changes, users will be notified via a banner on the website.
The date of the last update is indicated at the top of this document.

sections.title: Contact Information
content: Data Controller: NAKO Agency.
Email: hello@nakoagency.com.
Website: nakoagency.com.
For GDPR-specific inquiries, please write to: hello@nakoagency.com.

sections.title: General Provisions
content: These Terms of Use (hereinafter — "Terms") govern the use of the website nakoagency.com (hereinafter — "Website") operated by NAKO Agency (hereinafter — "Company", "we", "our").
By accessing and using the Website, you agree to these Terms. If you do not agree, please stop using the Website.

Website strategy and planning.

Design, layout, and visual elements.
Texts, images, and graphics.

sections.title: Service Delivery Procedure

Application through the Website form or email.

Signing of the service agreement.

Specific conditions, timelines, and costs are determined individually for each project.

sections.title: Payment for Services

Payment is made according to the terms specified in the agreement.
Standard payment scheme: 50% prepayment, 50% upon completion.
For large projects, payment may be split into 3 stages.
Payment methods are agreed upon individually.
Refund terms are specified in the agreement.

sections.title: Limitation of Liability
content: The Company shall not be liable for:
Temporary unavailability of the Website due to technical maintenance.
Losses arising from the use or inability to use the Website.
Actions of third parties, including hosting providers and payment systems.
Force majeure circumstances.
The Company's total liability is limited to the amount paid by the client under the agreement.

sections.title: Disclaimer of Warranties
content: The Website is provided "as is". The Company does not guarantee:
Uninterrupted Website operation.
Absence of technical errors.
Compliance of the Website with your specific requirements.
Accuracy and completeness of information posted on the Website.
We strive to ensure the highest quality but cannot guarantee the absence of any defects.

sections.title: Links to Third-Party Resources
content: The Website may contain links to third-party websites. We are not responsible for:
Content of third-party websites.
Privacy policies of third-party resources.
Any losses arising from the use of third-party resources.

sections.title: Changes to Terms
content: We reserve the right to modify these Terms at any time without prior notice. Changes take effect upon publication on the Website.
Continued use of the Website after changes have been made constitutes your acceptance of the new Terms.
The date of the last update is indicated at the top of the document.

sections.title: Applicable Law and Jurisdiction
content: These Terms are governed by the laws applicable in the user's jurisdiction.
For EU residents (Romania, Poland, Germany, Italy): disputes are subject to GDPR and the courts of the user's EU member state.
For Moldovan residents: governed by the laws of the Republic of Moldova.
For Ukrainian residents: governed by the laws of Ukraine.
For Russian residents: governed by the laws of the Russian Federation. Disputes are resolved in courts of the Russian Federation.
For Kazakh residents: governed by the laws of the Republic of Kazakhstan.
In all other cases: governed by the laws of Romania as the Company's principal place of operation.

sections.title: Age Restrictions
content: By using this Website, you confirm that you meet the minimum age requirement in your jurisdiction:
Germany, Netherlands: 16 years.
Italy, France: 14 years.
Poland, Romania, Moldova, Ukraine: 13 years.
Russia, Kazakhstan: 14 years.
All other countries: 13 years.
If you are under the required age, you must obtain parental consent before using this Website.

sections.title: Contact Information
content: If you have questions regarding these Terms, contact us:
Email: hello@nakoagency.com
Website: nakoagency.com

cookiePolicy.metaDescription: NAKO Agency cookie policy. Detailed information about cookies we use, their purposes and how to manage them.
cookiePolicy.caption: Legal information

cookieTable.name: _ga
cookieTable.purpose: Distinguishes unique users by assigning a randomly generated number as a client identifier

cookieTable.name: _ga_*
cookieTable.purpose: Maintains session state and tracks user interactions across pages

cookieTable.name: NEXT_LOCALE
cookieTable.purpose: Stores the user's preferred language for the website interface

cookieTable.name: user-region
cookieTable.purpose: Stores the detected or selected user region for localized content and pricing

cookieTable.name: cc_status
cookieTable.purpose: Stores the user's cookie consent preferences and choices

cookieTable.name: _vercel_*
cookieTable.purpose: Used for platform analytics, performance monitoring and deployment optimization

sections.title: What Are Cookies
content: Cookies are small text files that are stored on your device when you visit a website. They help the website remember your preferences and improve your browsing experience.
Cookies can be "session" cookies, which are deleted when you close your browser, or "persistent" cookies, which remain on your device for a set period of time.

sections.title: How We Use Cookies
content: We use cookies to ensure the proper functioning of our website, to analyze traffic and user behavior, and to remember your language and region preferences.
Cookies also help us understand which pages are most popular and how visitors navigate the site, allowing us to continuously improve our services.

sections.title: Cookie Categories
content: Necessary cookies are essential for the website to function properly. They enable core features such as language selection, cookie consent storage, and security.
Analytics cookies help us understand how visitors interact with our website by collecting and reporting information anonymously.
Preferences cookies allow the website to remember your choices such as region and display settings to provide a more personalized experience.
Marketing cookies are used to track visitors across websites to display relevant advertisements. We currently do not use marketing cookies.

sections.title: Managing Cookies
content: When you first visit our website, a cookie banner will appear allowing you to accept or reject non-essential cookies. You can change your preferences at any time through the cookie settings link in the footer.
You can also manage cookies through your browser settings. Most browsers allow you to block or delete cookies. Please note that disabling certain cookies may affect website functionality.

sections.title: Third-Party Cookies
content: We use Google Analytics to analyze website traffic. Google may set cookies on your device to collect anonymous usage data. For more information, visit Google's privacy policy.
Our website is hosted on Vercel, which may set technical cookies necessary for content delivery and platform performance.

sections.title: Contact

website-cost-for-business-2026.title: How Much Does a Business Website Cost in 2026
website-cost-for-business-2026.excerpt: What the price is made up of, what businesses overpay for, and how to calculate a budget so the website pays for itself in 2–3 months.
website-cost-for-business-2026.author: NAKO Agency

toc.title: Why “how much does a website cost” is the wrong question
What businesses actually pay for
Hidden costs nobody warns you about
What happens when you cut corners on the wrong things
How to budget so the website pays for itself
5 signs your website is already costing you money

website-cost-for-business-2026.content:
Why “how much does a website cost” is the wrong question

If you google “website cost,” you’ll get numbers from $300 to $100,000. And they’re all true. Because “website” is like “renovation”: you can put up wallpaper over the weekend, or you can rebuild an entire apartment turnkey. Both are “renovation.” But the result and lifespan are completely different.

Most business owners don’t understand what exactly they’re paying for. The result: they either overpay for things they don’t need, or cut costs where cutting is dangerous.

If you’re currently comparing proposals from different contractors and can’t understand why prices differ by a factor of 10 — this article is for you.

What businesses actually pay for

Website type. A one-page landing, a corporate site with 10–15 pages, and an online store with a catalog — these are three different projects. Different volumes of design, development, and content.

Custom design vs. template. A $50 template doesn’t account for your audience or goals. It looks “fine,” but it doesn’t sell. Custom design is built around a specific objective: a lead, a call, a purchase.

Functionality. Forms, CRM integrations, online payment, multilingual support — each feature adds hours of work. But not every feature is needed at launch.

Content. Copy, photos, video. Clients often think their content is “already done.” Then it turns out the phone photos don’t cut it, and the text reads: “About us: we are a dynamically developing...” Quality content is a separate budget item.

Hidden costs nobody warns you about

Here’s what typically stays outside the proposal:

Hosting and domain — $50 to $300 per year. A small thing, but over 3 years it adds up noticeably.

SSL certificate — without one, the browser shows visitors “Not Secure.” Free options exist, but an online store needs an extended one.

Maintenance and updates — a website doesn’t work on a “build it and forget it” principle. The CMS needs updates, bugs need fixing, content needs refreshing.

SEO optimization — a website without SEO is a shop in a basement with no signage. Basic optimization at launch starts at $500.

If the proposal doesn’t mention any of these items — ask about them. Otherwise they’ll be a surprise after launch.

What happens when you cut corners on the wrong things

A real-world scenario. A business ordered a website for $800 from a freelancer. Six months later:

  • The site takes 6 seconds to load — according to Google, more than half of mobile users leave before a page finishes loading
  • On mobile, buttons overlap each other — impossible to use
  • The site doesn’t rank in search — there was no SEO
  • The freelancer stopped responding — no one to fix things

Total: $800 spent, zero leads, needs a complete redo. The redo cost more than doing it right in the first place.

A different scenario. A business invested $5,000 in a well-thought-out website with a clear goal — leads from search. Six months later:

  • The site is in the top 10 for key search queries
  • Conversion rate of 4–6% — every 20th visitor submits a lead
  • Cost per lead from the website is 3x lower than from ads

The takeaway is simple: the difference isn’t in the budget. The difference is whether the website was built around a business objective or just “to have one.”

How to budget so the website pays for itself

Instead of asking “how much does a website cost,” ask a different question: “how much will one client from this website cost?”

If your average deal is $2,000, and the website brings in 10 leads per month with every fifth one closing — that’s $4,000 in revenue. A $5,000 website pays for itself in 2 months.

Think return, not expense. A website isn’t a cost. It’s a sales tool with measurable ROI.

Price benchmarks in 2026 (vary by niche, region, and requirements):

  • Landing page (1 page) — $1,500–3,000
  • Corporate website (5–15 pages) — $3,000–8,000
  • Online store — $5,000–15,000
  • Complex project with integrations — from $10,000

5 signs your website is already costing you money

If you recognize yourself in even one of these — the website isn’t just “needed.” It’s already costing you dearly:

  1. You don’t have a website, and clients only come through word of mouth — you’re dependent on a single channel
  2. You have a website but you’re embarrassed to share the link — which means clients feel it too
  3. You’re investing in advertising but getting few leads — the traffic may be leaking at the website stage
  4. Competitors look more professional online — clients choose them instead
  5. You don’t know how many clients you’re losing — which means you’re not managing the process

The website costs you money not because you pay for it. But because you’re not getting what you could be getting every month.

what-website-your-business-needs.title: What Type of Website Your Business Needs: Landing Page, Multi-Page Site, or Online Store
what-website-your-business-needs.excerpt: Choosing the wrong website format is one of the most expensive mistakes. Let’s figure out which type fits your sales model.
what-website-your-business-needs.author: NAKO Agency

toc.title: The most expensive mistake — building the wrong website
Landing page: one page, one goal
Multi-page site: when trust matters more than speed
Online store: when you sell products online
How to determine your format: 5 questions
Three signs the format was chosen wrong

what-website-your-business-needs.content:
The most expensive mistake — building the wrong website

A business with 50 products orders a landing page. A consultant with a single service builds a 20-page multi-page site. The result is the same: money spent, and the website doesn’t solve the problem.

Website format isn’t a matter of taste or budget. It’s a strategic decision that depends on how you sell, who your client is, and where your traffic comes from.

If you’re currently planning a website and aren’t sure about the format — read to the end. This might save you several thousand dollars.

Landing page: one page, one goal

For whom: one product or service, a clear offer, traffic from ads.

A landing page is a single page with a single goal: get a lead. No menu, no navigation, no distractions. Arrived → understood → left contact info.

When it works:

  • You’re promoting a specific product or offer
  • Traffic comes from paid advertising
  • The decision is made quickly — booking, order, call
  • The client doesn’t need to study your company in depth

When it doesn’t work:

  • You have 5+ services or product categories
  • Clients think for weeks before buying
  • You need to show case studies, expertise, your team

Real-world example. A nail technician: one studio, clear pricing, online booking. A landing page is the perfect format. But if they have 3 locations and 10 services, a landing page is no longer enough.

Multi-page site: when trust matters more than speed

For whom: services, B2B, experts, companies with multiple service lines.

When the average deal is high, the client doesn’t buy on first contact. They need to study you: see case studies, read about your approach, get to know the team.

When it works:

  • The client compares 3–5 options before deciding
  • You have multiple services or business lines
  • Expertise matters: law, consulting, healthcare, education
  • You need organic search traffic — each page serves as an entry point

What pages are needed in practice:

  • Home — first impression and navigation
  • Services — what you do and for whom
  • Cases — proof of results
  • About — the people behind the business
  • Contacts — how to get in touch
  • Blog (optional) — SEO and demonstrating expertise

Example. An architecture firm with 4 service lines, a portfolio of 20 projects, and clients who spend months choosing a contractor. A landing page won’t cut it here — a full-fledged website is needed.

If you have multiple services but everything is packed into one page — clients most likely can’t find what they’re looking for. And they leave.

Online store: when you sell products online

For whom: retail, physical goods with delivery, digital products.

Not a landing page with a “message us on WhatsApp” button, but a full system: catalog, cart, payment, inventory tracking.

Signs you need a store:

  • More than 10 products
  • Clients can buy without a consultation
  • You need online payment
  • You want to scale without growing headcount

Important note. An online store isn’t just a “catalog on a website.” It’s a system that includes logistics, payment processing, inventory management, and marketing. If you’re not ready for that yet — start with a multi-page site with a catalog and an order form. It’s cheaper and faster.

How to determine your format: 5 questions

  1. How many products/services do you have? 1–2 → landing page. 3–10 → multi-page site. 10+ products → store.
  2. How does the client make a decision? Quickly → landing page. Slowly, comparing options → multi-page site.
  3. Where does traffic come from? Ads → landing page. Search → multi-page site. Marketplaces + own channel → store.
  4. Do you need online payment? Yes → store. No → landing page or multi-page site.
  5. What’s the budget? Minimal → landing page. Mid-range → multi-page site. Large → store.

If the answers point in different directions — that’s normal. Format depends on dozens of factors that don’t fit into a single checklist. The key is not to guess, but to make a decision based on data about your business.

Three signs the website format was chosen wrong

  1. You’re spending on ads but conversion is below 2% — the format may not match your sales model
  2. Clients call with questions that should be answered on the website — which means there’s not enough information
  3. You feel the website doesn’t match the quality of your services — clients feel this even more acutely

The right format isn’t about website size. It’s about how precisely the website answers your client’s questions and guides them toward action.

why-ads-fail-without-good-website.title: Why Advertising Doesn’t Work Without a Proper Website
why-ads-fail-without-good-website.excerpt: Ad budget keeps growing but leads stay the same? Most likely, traffic is leaking at the website stage. We show you exactly where.
why-ads-fail-without-good-website.author: NAKO Agency

toc.title: Where your ad budget is leaking
Where the website drains your ad budget
The difference in numbers: same budget, different results
What a website needs to look like for ads to pay off
Checklist: is your website ready for advertising?
What to do about it

why-ads-fail-without-good-website.content:
Where your ad budget is leaking

You’re investing $2,000 a month. Clicks are coming in, reach is growing. But leads? 5–10. Cost per lead: $200–400. With an average deal of $1,000, the ads barely break even.

The first reaction is “the ads don’t work” or “the marketer is bad.” But often the problem is elsewhere: the ads bring people in, and the website scares them off.

It’s like renting a billboard by the highway, but instead of a store there’s a locked door with a sign that says “call us.” Traffic exists. Sales don’t.

If you’ve already run ads and were disappointed by the results — check whether the website is the problem.

Where the website drains your ad budget

Slow loading.

According to Google’s research, when loading takes longer than 3 seconds, more than half of mobile users leave. You’ve already paid for the click — but the person didn’t even see your offer. Every extra second isn’t a technical nuance, it’s lost money.

The site isn’t mobile-friendly.

70–80% of ad traffic is mobile. If the text is tiny on a phone, buttons don’t respond, and the page shifts around — the visitor closes the tab in 3 seconds. They won’t figure it out. They’ll go to a competitor whose site is convenient.

If you haven’t opened your website on a phone in a while — do it right now. You might see what your potential clients see.

Disconnect between the ad and the website.

Someone clicked an ad that said “Custom kitchens from $3,000.” But the website says “comprehensive solutions for your interior.” No prices, no examples, no specifics. The ad’s promise wasn’t confirmed — trust is lost.

What this looks like in practice. A business ran ads for apartment renovation. The ad said “turnkey renovation, estimate in 2 hours.” The website had a generic “About us” page with no prices, no portfolio, no estimate form. Result: 200 clicks, 2 leads. Conversion: 1%.

No clear next step.

The visitor read everything, they’re interested — but what now? If the “Submit a request” button is buried at the bottom of the page and the form is on a separate tab — the person just leaves. Not because they don’t want to, but because they didn’t understand what to do.

No reason to trust.

No reviews, no case studies, no team photos, no legal information. To a new visitor, your website is a stranger on the internet. And people don’t give their contact info to strangers.

The difference in numbers: same budget, different results

Let’s say ad traffic is 1,000 people per month, cost per click is $2.

Website with problems (conversion ~1%):

  • Leads: 10
  • Cost per lead: $200

Solid website (conversion ~4%):

  • Leads: 40
  • Cost per lead: $50

The difference is 4x. Same ad budget. The only variable: website quality.

Over a year, that’s 120 vs. 480 leads. With a 20% close rate and an average deal of $2,000, the difference is between $48,000 and $192,000 in revenue. Exact numbers depend on the niche, but the magnitude is telling.

Takeaway: before increasing your ad budget, make sure the website isn’t wasting what you already have.

What a website needs to look like for ads to pay off

Fast. Under 2 seconds on mobile. Research shows that each additional second of loading can reduce conversion by 5–7%.

Consistent. If the ad says “Custom kitchens from $3,000,” the website should show kitchens, prices, and examples. The page should continue the conversation the ad started.

Clear. Headline → benefits → proof → action. In 10 seconds, the visitor should understand: what you offer, why they can trust you, and what to do next.

With clear CTAs. A “Submit a request” button on every screen. A simple form, 3–4 fields max.

Checklist: is your website ready for advertising?

  • ☐ Loads in 2–3 seconds on mobile
  • ☐ Displays correctly on a phone
  • ☐ Headline matches the ad’s promise
  • ☐ Prices or price ranges are shown
  • ☐ Reviews or case studies with results are present
  • ☐ CTA button is visible without scrolling
  • ☐ The contact form works and submits
  • ☐ Contact details and legal information are listed

If more than 3 items are unchecked — the site isn’t ready for ads. Every dollar you invest is working at half capacity — and that’s not an ad problem, it’s a landing page problem.

What to do about it

Sometimes a few targeted tweaks are enough: speed up loading, rewrite the headline, add a form above the fold. Sometimes you need a new website — because the old one can’t be restructured.

Figuring out what you need takes about 15 minutes. Just go through your site with your client’s eyes — or ask someone who’s seeing it for the first time.

how-many-leads-your-business-loses.title: How Many Leads Your Business Loses Because of a Weak Website
how-many-leads-your-business-loses.excerpt: Every day your website either brings in clients or quietly loses them. We calculate the losses in dollars — and show you what to do about it.
how-many-leads-your-business-loses.author: NAKO Agency

toc.title: Why you don’t notice you’re losing clients
Why people leave even when they need your product
How much this costs in dollars
7 signs your website is working against you
What a strong website actually changes
Where to start right now

how-many-leads-your-business-loses.content:
Why you don’t notice you’re losing clients

When a store is empty, you can see it. When a sales rep is doing poorly, you can hear it. But when a website is losing clients — nobody calls to say: “I wanted to buy from you, but your website scared me off.”

People simply close the tab. And you’ll never know they were there.

According to averaged analytics data, a typical small business website converts 2–5% of visitors. The remaining 95–98% leave. Some left because they don’t need your product. But some left because the website didn’t convince them. And that portion is your lost money.

Why people leave even when they need your product

The first 3 seconds decide everything.

A person opens the site and within 3 seconds decides: stay or close. If they didn’t understand what you do and how you can help — they leave. Not to a competitor. Just out of your funnel.

Appearance = level of trust.

A Stanford University study found that roughly 3 out of 4 users judge a company’s credibility by its website’s appearance. Outdated design says: “this company isn’t growing.” Or worse: “something’s off here.”

If your website is over 3 years old and hasn’t been updated — clients notice. Even if they can’t pinpoint exactly what’s wrong.

Slow loading silently cuts you off.

A site that loads in 5 seconds instead of 2 can lose up to 20% of potential leads — simply because people don’t want to wait. And you won’t even know: in analytics, these visitors show up as a “bounce.”

Mobile version is not optional, it’s essential.

Over 60% of traffic comes from phones. A non-responsive site means losing more than half your audience. If buttons are tiny, text is unreadable, and the page needs pinch-zooming — the visitor leaves.

No reason to leave contact info.

No reviews, no case studies, no faces. To a new visitor, you’re one of thousands in the search results. Without proof of your work, they won’t leave their number.

How much this costs in dollars

A simple formula. You need three numbers:

  1. Traffic — how many people visit the website per month
  2. Conversion — the percentage who submit a lead
  3. Average deal size — how much one client brings in

Example for a business with 500 monthly visitors:

At 1% conversion (5 leads), closing every third one, with a $3,000 deal:

  • 1–2 clients → $3,000–6,000/mo

At 4% conversion (20 leads):

  • 6–7 clients → $18,000–21,000/mo

The difference: $12,000–15,000 per month. Same traffic. Same product. Same market. One thing changed — website quality.

Exact numbers depend on the niche, seasonality, and offer. But the magnitude of difference between a “weak” and a “decent” website is exactly this.

7 signs your website is working against you

1. You’re embarrassed to share the link. If you yourself know the site is “meh” — clients feel it ten times more.

2. The site is over 3 years old and hasn’t been touched. The web changes fast. What looked fine in 2023 looks outdated in 2026.

3. It’s unusable on a phone. Tiny text, horizontal scrolling, invisible buttons.

4. The first screen doesn’t explain what you do. 5 seconds — and the decision is made. If the offer isn’t clear by then, the visitor is gone.

5. No case studies or reviews. The words “we’re professionals” without proof are empty noise.

6. The contact form is hidden or broken. If you need to scroll through 10 screens to reach the form, or it doesn’t submit — you’re losing people who were already ready.

7. You don’t know your conversion rate. If you’re not measuring how many leads the site generates — you’re not managing your online sales.

What a strong website actually changes

A strong website isn’t a “pretty” one. It’s a website that works for the business:

  • Loads fast and works well on any device
  • Explains what you do and for whom within 5 seconds
  • Shows proof: case studies, reviews, numbers
  • Guides toward action: simple form, visible button, chat
  • Builds trust: design, content, legal information

Where to start right now

Open your website on your phone. Time the loading. Try to understand the offer in 5 seconds. Try to submit a lead.

If something goes wrong at any step — you’ve already found where clients are leaking.

From there, two paths: fix it yourself or show it to a specialist. Sometimes 2–3 tweaks are enough. Sometimes you need a new site. But the first step is seeing the problem.

how-to-choose-web-contractor.title: How to Choose a Web Contractor Without Losing Money, Deadlines, or Your Sanity
how-to-choose-web-contractor.excerpt: Freelancer, studio, or agency? We break down the red flags, portfolio evaluation rules, and what absolutely must be in the contract.
how-to-choose-web-contractor.author: NAKO Agency

toc.title: Why choosing a contractor is stressful
Freelancer, studio, or agency: an honest comparison
How to evaluate a portfolio without being deceived
Red flags: what to watch out for
What absolutely must be in the contract
Signs of a contractor worth working with
Three things to do before making your choice

how-to-choose-web-contractor.content:
Why choosing a website contractor is stressful

Thousands of providers, similar portfolios, identical promises: “we’ll make it beautiful, fast, and affordable.” But behind the nice words often hide missed deadlines, surprise charges, and results that fall far short of expectations.

The core problem is that you don’t choose contractors every month. It’s a one-time purchase. And the mistake costs not just money, but time — which is often worth more.

If you’re currently comparing proposals and don’t know who to trust with your project — this article will help you filter out the unreliable ones and choose those worth working with.

Freelancer, studio, or agency: an honest comparison

Freelancer ($500–3,000)

Pros: cheapest option, quick start, direct communication.

Cons: one person does everything (design, code, content), may disappear, no legal guarantees.

Best for: simple landing pages, MVPs, when budget is minimal and the task is straightforward.

Studio ($3,000–15,000)

Pros: a team (designer + developer + project manager), established processes, portfolio.

Cons: more expensive than a freelancer, sometimes a “conveyor belt” approach — making similar sites for everyone.

Best for: corporate websites, online stores, businesses with clear requirements.

Agency ($10,000+)

Pros: strategy + marketing + development, deep immersion in the client’s business.

Cons: expensive, long approval chains.

Best for: complex projects, rebrands, when you need not just “a website” but a marketing tool.

There’s no “best” format. There’s the format that fits your task, budget, and expectations. A consultant with one service doesn’t have to go to an agency. And a company with 5 service lines shouldn’t take a risk with a freelancer.

How to evaluate a portfolio without being deceived

Open the actual websites, not just screenshots.

A screenshot can be photoshopped. A real website can’t. Open every project from the portfolio. Does it load? Does it work on mobile? Does it look current? If the contractor shows images but the real sites don’t work — that’s a serious signal.

Look for variety.

If all the sites in the portfolio look the same — yours will too. A good contractor adapts their approach to each client’s task, not churning out copies.

Ask about results, not aesthetics.

A beautiful website ≠ an effective one. Ask: what business results did this site deliver? How many leads? How did conversion change? If the contractor doesn’t know, they’re thinking about design, not your business.

Ask about the process.

How was the work structured? How long did the project take? Were there problems and how were they solved? The answers reveal a contractor’s maturity better than any portfolio.

Red flags: what to watch out for

“We’ll do it in 3 days.” A proper website doesn’t get built in 3 days. If they promise that — it’ll be a template with swapped text, without considering your audience or goals.

No contract. “Let’s skip the paperwork, we trust each other” — no. A contract protects both parties. Without one, you have no legal leverage.

100% upfront payment. The standard is 50/50 or milestone-based payments. Full prepayment is the risk of losing money if something goes wrong.

They don’t ask questions. If the contractor doesn’t ask about your audience, competitors, and business goals — they’ll build “a website in general,” not a website for your business.

“About a month or so.” That’s not a timeline. There should be specific milestones with dates: design by this date, development by that date, launch on this date.

They guarantee business results. “#1 on Google” or “100 leads in the first month” — that’s a lie. An honest contractor will tell you what they can do on their end, while acknowledging that results depend on joint effort.

If you spot 2+ red flags — keep looking. Even if the price is attractive.

What absolutely must be in the contract

Deadlines. Specific dates. What happens if there’s a delay — on both sides.

Scope of work. A clear list: number of pages, features, what’s included in the price and what isn’t.

Cost and payment schedule. Fixed price or hourly? What milestones? What’s billed separately?

Rights to deliverables. Code, design, content — everything should transfer to you upon full payment.

Post-launch support. Warranty period? Cost of additional work? Who maintains the site?

Acceptance process. How many rounds of revisions are included? What counts as a “completed project”?

Signs of a contractor worth working with

  • Asks many questions before starting — wants to understand your task
  • Shows real, working projects — not just screenshots
  • Is upfront about what’s included in the price and what isn’t
  • Provides specific timelines and milestones
  • Knows how to say “no” — if the task can’t be done within your budget
  • Thinks about business results, not just design
  • Has testimonials from real clients

Three things to do before making your choice

  1. Describe your task in writing. Not “I need a website,” but “I need a website to generate leads for renovation services in [city], budget X, launch in Y.” The more specific the brief, the more accurate the estimate.
  2. Request proposals from 3–5 contractors. Compare not just prices, but approach: who asked questions? Who proposed a solution? Who just named a price?
  3. Check real websites from their portfolio. Open them, click around, evaluate speed and usability. This tells you more than any presentation.

Choosing a contractor isn’t about the lowest price. It’s about trust, communication, and understanding your task. The one who builds the wrong thing will cost you more than the one who builds it right.

ceramo-lab.title: Ceramo Lab — Ceramic Granite Laboratory
ceramo-lab.description: Corporate website for a ceramic granite manufacturer. Minimalist design, video backgrounds, GSAP animations.
ceramo-lab.task: Create a premium corporate website for a company working with ceramic granite as an architectural material. The site should convey a laboratory approach to the material and showcase two directions: product manufacturing and installation/mounting.
solution: Developed a minimalist design focused on ceramic granite textures
Implemented fullscreen video backgrounds for material immersion
Built a scroll animation system using GSAP + ScrollTrigger
Designed a two-column layout for two business directions
Created a 4-step approach timeline with animated connecting line
Optimized performance: lazy loading, video and image optimization

mindjoy.title: MindJoy — Educational Platform
mindjoy.description: Educational platform for kids with gamification, AI personalization, and parental controls.
mindjoy.task: Create a landing page for MindJoy, an educational platform that turns learning for kids aged 4–14 into exciting adventures. The site should convince parents of the safety and effectiveness of the approach, showcase key features, and convert visitors into app installations.
solution: Designed a dark UI system with gradients and neon accents — conveys a digital adventure atmosphere and differentiates the platform from competitors
Implemented a responsive grid of 6 feature cards with iconography and micro-animations on hover — each card reveals a key product function
Built an interactive 3-step onboarding block with visual markers and smooth transitions — lowers the entry barrier for new users
Integrated a social proof block: ratings, avatars, and real parent testimonials with reveal animations
Developed a live statistics section with animated counters (10,000+ families, 500,000+ missions, 50M+ XP, 45+ countries) — builds trust through scale
Configured full multilingual support (RU/EN) with server-side localization and SEO optimization for each language
